verses.text: And the LORD sent Nathan unto David. And he came unto him, and said unto him, There were two men in one city; the one rich, and the other poor. The rich man had exceeding many flocks and herds: But the poor man had nothing, save one little ewe lamb, which he had bought and nourished up: and it grew up together with him, and with his children; it did eat of his own meat, and drank of his own cup, and lay in his bosom, and was unto him as a daughter. And there came a traveller unto the rich man, and he spared to take of his own flock and of his own herd, to dress for the wayfaring man that was come unto him; but took the poor man's lamb, and dressed it for the man that was come to him. Guidance: 'The LORD sent Nathan.' The chapter opens by naming the divine action. Nathan does not arrive on his own initiative; he is dispatched. The LORD's verdict at the end of chapter 11 — 'the thing that David had done displeased the LORD' — is now translated into a sent prophet. Nathan does not lead with accusation; he leads with a parable. The story has four characters: a rich man (many flocks), a poor man (one little ewe lamb, raised as a family pet — eating, drinking from his cup, sleeping in his bosom, treated 'as a daughter'), a traveler (the guest demanding hospitality), and the killed lamb. The rich man, with abundant alternative animals to serve hospitality, chooses to take the poor man's only lamb. The story is set up as a legal case for the king to judge — Israelite kings adjudicated property disputes among their subjects regularly. Nathan is bringing what looks like a normal case. The genius of the parable is its pacing. Nathan keeps the analogy hidden long enough for David's righteous indignation to harden. The chapter is teaching us that the most effective prophetic confrontation is not the one that opens with the accusation; it is the one that lets the sinner condemn himself first.

Guidance: 'The LORD sent Nathan.' The chapter opens by naming the divine action. Nathan does not arrive on his own initiative; he is dispatched. The LORD's verdict at the end of chapter 11 — 'the thing that David had done displeased the LORD' — is now translated into a sent prophet. Nathan does not lead with accusation; he leads with a parable. The story has four characters: a rich man (many flocks), a poor man (one little ewe lamb, raised as a family pet — eating, drinking from his cup, sleeping in his bosom, treated 'as a daughter'), a traveler (the guest demanding hospitality), and the killed lamb. And David's anger was greatly kindled against the man; and he said to Nathan, As the LORD liveth, the man that hath done this thing shall surely die: And he shall restore the lamb fourfold, because he did this thing, and because he had no pity. Guidance: David's reaction is what Nathan was waiting for. 'David's anger was greatly kindled' — the king has been listening as the kingdom's chief judge, and the case before him outrages him. He delivers the verdict in two parts. (1) 'As the LORD liveth, the man that hath done this thing shall surely die.' Capital sentence — though strictly speaking Exodus 22:1 calls for fourfold restitution for stolen sheep, not death; David's anger has driven him beyond Torah penalty. (2) 'He shall restore the lamb fourfold' — the Torah penalty added on top of the capital sentence. The detail is precise. David has just condemned himself unknowingly to fourfold loss. The next chapters of 2 Samuel will give us the four: the baby of Bathsheba (chapter 12), Amnon (chapter 13), Absalom (chapter 18), Adoni-jah (1 Kings 2). Four sons of David will die. The fourfold restitution David spoke against an imaginary lamb-stealer is what the LORD will collect from his own household. There is a category of judgment in which the angry verdict the sinner pronounces against another's sin is the verdict God then collects against the sinner. The most consequential sentence David has spoken in his life has just escaped his lips, and he does not yet know who he sentenced.

And Nathan said to David, Thou art the man. Thus saith the LORD God of Israel, I anointed thee king over Israel, and I delivered thee out of the hand of Saul; And I gave thee thy master's house, and thy master's wives into thy bosom, and gave thee the house of Israel and of Judah; and if that had been too little, I would moreover have given unto thee such and such things. Guidance: 'Thou art the man.' Three words in English, two words in Hebrew (attah ha-ish). The most devastating prophetic sentence in the Old Testament. Nathan does not draw out the moment; he delivers the identification immediately and proceeds. Then the LORD's accusation, structured around what He has given David. (1) 'I anointed thee king over Israel.' The kingdom was a gift. (2) 'I delivered thee out of the hand of Saul.' The wilderness years' survival was a gift. (3) 'I gave thee thy master's house, and thy master's wives into thy bosom.' Saul's whole household, including (in the legal sense) the right to take Saul's widows, was a gift — though there is no record that David took Saul's wives biologically; the language is the ancient Near-Eastern formula for full succession. (4) 'I gave thee the house of Israel and of Judah.' The unified kingdom was a gift. (5) 'If that had been too little, I would moreover have given unto thee such and such things.' If you had wanted more, you could have asked Me. The LORD's complaint is that David took Bathsheba as if there were not enough provided already. The deepest sin is not lust per se; it is the heart that distrusts the LORD's generosity. The chapter is showing us that adultery, at its theological root, is the sin of someone who looked at what God had given and concluded it was not enough.

Wherefore hast thou despised the commandment of the LORD, to do evil in his sight? thou hast killed Uriah the Hittite with the sword, and hast taken his wife to be thy wife, and hast slain him with the sword of the children of Ammon. Now therefore the sword shall never depart from thine house; because thou hast despised me, and hast taken the wife of Uriah the Hittite to be thy wife. Guidance: 'Wherefore hast thou despised the commandment of the LORD?' The verb 'despised' (bazah) is strong — to regard with contempt, to count as worthless. The LORD identifies David's sin not just as transgression but as despite — the king valued his own desire over the LORD's clearly-known command. Then the specification: 'thou hast killed Uriah the Hittite with the sword, and hast taken his wife to be thy wife.' The narrator's careful prose: David killed Uriah and took his wife. Uriah's death is named first, even though chronologically it followed the adultery. The murder is the cap of the chain. The 'sword of the children of Ammon' — Nathan diagnoses Joab's tactical maneuver of placing Uriah where Ammonite swordsmen would kill him. The whole apparatus of cover-up has been seen and is now named. Then the sentence: 'Now therefore the sword shall never depart from thine house.' The same sword David used to kill Uriah will now never leave David's family. The sentence is generational. Amnon will be killed by Absalom's swordsmen (13:29); Absalom will be killed in the battle (18:14); Adonijah will be killed by Solomon's command (1 Kgs 2:25). The sword that took Uriah is going to take David's own sons. There is a category of consequence that runs through the perpetrator's children. David has set in motion something that will outlast his own life.

Thus saith the LORD, Behold, I will raise up evil against thee out of thine own house, and I will take thy wives before thine eyes, and give them unto thy neighbour, and he shall lie with thy wives in the sight of this sun. For thou didst it secretly: but I will do this thing before all Israel, and before the sun. Guidance: The sentence continues. 'I will raise up evil against thee out of thine own house.' The threat will come from inside David's own family, not from foreign enemies. Absalom's rebellion (chapters 15-18) is the precise fulfillment — the son rises against the father; the wives David has been keeping in Jerusalem are taken by Absalom in chapter 16 ('Absalom went in unto his father's concubines in the sight of all Israel,' 16:22). The geography is also precise: Absalom will pitch a tent on the same roof David walked when he saw Bathsheba bathing. The very rooftop will host the public version of the same sin. 'In the sight of this sun' — that is, in public, in broad daylight. 'For thou didst it secretly: but I will do this thing before all Israel, and before the sun.' The principle: hidden sin will produce public consequences. The chapter is teaching one of the great Old Testament lessons: God's justice tends to render exposed what the sinner had concealed. The discrepancy between the secret act and the public consequence will, in time, be closed. David's secret roof becomes Absalom's public tent. The chapter does not pretend this is anything other than awful.

And David said unto Nathan, I have sinned against the LORD. And Nathan said unto David, The LORD also hath put away thy sin; thou shalt not die. Guidance: Four words in Hebrew: chatati la-YHWH. 'I have sinned against the LORD.' Compare Saul's three confessions in 1 Samuel (15:24, 15:30, 26:21), each of which was followed by self-justification or political face-saving. David's confession has no qualifier. He does not say 'I have sinned because of the people, the wine, the moment.' He simply names the offense and names the party offended. The brevity is what makes it work. Compare also Psalm 51, David's poetic version of this same confession ('against thee, thee only, have I sinned, and done this evil in thy sight') — the same theological recognition that the deepest hurt of sin is the offense against God Himself. Nathan's response is one of the great pastoral lines in scripture: 'The LORD also hath put away thy sin; thou shalt not die.' Forgiveness is announced. The Torah penalty for adultery (Lev 20:10) and murder (Num 35:31) was death; David is being told he is forgiven the eternal-consequence of those sins. He will live; the LORD has put away his iniquity. The chapter is showing us the speed of God's mercy for genuine confession. David said 'I have sinned' and immediately heard 'thy sin is put away.' There was no extended waiting period, no penance to complete, no proof of sincerity to deliver. The grace is fast. But the chapter is going to be careful: forgiveness of the eternal-consequence does not erase the temporal-consequences. The sword on his house, the rebellion from his son, the child about to die — these will all still come. God's forgiveness covers the relational rupture between Him and David; it does not unwind the historical events David's sin set in motion. The chapter is teaching us the precise theology of forgiveness: God forgives the sin and absorbs the deepest consequence (death); the world still works out the temporal consequences (the sword, the rebellion). Christians read this through the cross — Jesus, the Son of David, takes the eternal weight; the believer still walks through the historical aftermath. David is forgiven; David is also going to lose four sons.

Howbeit, because by this deed thou hast given great occasion to the enemies of the LORD to blaspheme, the child also that is born unto thee shall surely die. And Nathan departed unto his house. Guidance: The first of the four sons. 'Because by this deed thou hast given great occasion to the enemies of the LORD to blaspheme.' The reason given is unexpected. Not 'because you sinned'; that has been forgiven. But because the sin gave the LORD's enemies cause to mock the LORD's name. The Israelite king's character had been the LORD's witness to surrounding nations — David's chesed toward Mephibosheth, his refusal to harm Saul, his dance before the ark, were all part of the LORD's reputation in the region. The Bathsheba episode has handed the LORD's enemies ammunition. The child of the adulterous union must die because the public consequence is part of what answers the public scandal. The chapter is teaching us a hard pastoral truth: the consequences of a public figure's sin are not just personal; they include the harm done to the LORD's reputation through the public figure's witness. Sometimes those consequences fall on the next generation. 'Nathan departed unto his house.' The prophet's job is finished. He has confronted, named the sentence, and pronounced the forgiveness. He goes home. The drama now moves to the household crisis.

And Nathan departed unto his house. And the LORD struck the child that Uriah's wife bare unto David, and it was very sick. David therefore besought God for the child; and David fasted, and went in, and lay all night upon the earth. And the elders of his house arose, and went to him, to raise him up from the earth: but he would not, neither did he eat bread with them. Guidance: The sentence begins to fall. 'The LORD struck the child' — sudden illness, probably an infection. Note the narrator's name for Bathsheba: 'Uriah's wife.' Even now, the LORD's text continues to call her by her first husband's name. The murder is not forgotten in the divine record. David's response is desperate intercession. He fasts, goes 'in' (probably his private chamber), lies on the bare ground (the posture of mourning and intense prayer), and stays there for seven days. The senior household servants try to lift him up, to comfort him, to get him to eat. He refuses. The chapter is showing us a David who, having received Nathan's word that the child will die, still believes that the LORD might relent. The intercession is the act of a man who has known the LORD's mercies long enough to know that the LORD sometimes pivots. There is no contradiction between accepting the prophetic word and praying as if it might still change. Hezekiah will do the same in 2 Kgs 20:1-6 — given a prophetic word of his impending death, he prays anyway, and the LORD grants him fifteen more years. David is doing the same kind of intercessory work. He will lose this prayer; the chapter is going to show him losing it. But the wrestling is the right wrestling.

And it came to pass on the seventh day, that the child died. And the servants of David feared to tell him that the child was dead: for they said, Behold, while the child was yet alive, we spake unto him, and he would not hearken unto our voice: how will he then vex himself, if we tell him that the child is dead? But when David saw that his servants whispered, David perceived that the child was dead: therefore David said unto his servants, Is the child dead? And they said, He is dead. Then David arose from the earth, and washed, and anointed himself, and changed his apparel, and came into the house of the LORD, and worshipped: then he came to his own house; and when he required, they set bread before him, and he did eat. Guidance: The seventh day, the child dies. The servants are afraid to tell David. Their reasoning is humane: 'if he wouldn't listen to us while the child was alive, what will he do when he hears the child has died?' They are picturing a more catastrophic grief — perhaps self-harm. They whisper the news among themselves, expecting a delay. David perceives by their whispering. He asks directly: 'Is the child dead?' They say yes. The chapter pivots. David does the opposite of what the servants expected. (1) He rises from the ground. (2) He washes. (3) He anoints himself (oil — the standard post-mourning toilet). (4) He changes clothes. (5) He goes to the house of the LORD (the tent housing the ark, until Solomon's temple) and worships. (6) He returns home and eats. The servants are baffled. From inside, however, the move is consistent. David's intercession was for the LORD to relent; the LORD has not relented; the case is closed. David accepts the verdict and resumes life. The chapter is showing us one of the great pictures in scripture of how to receive a 'no' from God. Not by sulking, not by anger, not by leaving the worship community. By worshipping the God who said no. Worship of the LORD does not depend on the LORD's having said yes to the specific prayer.

And the servants of David feared to tell him that the child was dead: for they said, Behold, while the child was yet alive, we spake unto him, and he would not hearken unto our voice: how will he then vex himself, if we tell him that the child is dead? But when David saw that his servants whispered, David perceived that the child was dead: therefore David said unto his servants, Is the child dead? And they said, He is dead. Then David arose from the earth, and washed, and anointed himself, and changed his apparel, and came into the house of the LORD, and worshipped: then he came to his own house; and when he required, they set bread before him, and he did eat. Guidance: The seventh day, the child dies. The servants are afraid to tell David. Their reasoning is humane: 'if he wouldn't listen to us while the child was alive, what will he do when he hears the child has died?' They are picturing a more catastrophic grief — perhaps self-harm. They whisper the news among themselves, expecting a delay. David perceives by their whispering. He asks directly: 'Is the child dead?' They say yes. The chapter pivots. David does the opposite of what the servants expected. (1) He rises from the ground. (2) He washes. (3) He anoints himself (oil — the standard post-mourning toilet). (4) He changes clothes. (5) He goes to the house of the LORD (the tent housing the ark, until Solomon's temple) and worships. (6) He returns home and eats. The servants are baffled. From inside, however, the move is consistent. David's intercession was for the LORD to relent; the LORD has not relented; the case is closed. David accepts the verdict and resumes life. The chapter is showing us one of the great pictures in scripture of how to receive a 'no' from God. Not by sulking, not by anger, not by leaving the worship community. By worshipping the God who said no. Worship of the LORD does not depend on the LORD's having said yes to the specific prayer.

Then said his servants unto him, What thing is this that thou hast done? thou didst fast and weep for the child, while it was alive; but when the child was dead, thou didst rise and eat bread. And he said, While the child was yet alive, I fasted and wept: for I said, Who can tell whether GOD will be gracious to me, that the child may live? But now he is dead, wherefore should I fast? can I bring him back again? I shall go to him, but he shall not return to me. Guidance: The servants ask the natural question. David explains. 'While the child was yet alive, I fasted and wept: for I said, Who can tell whether GOD will be gracious to me, that the child may live?' The intercession had a contingency — 'who can tell' — and it had a hope — 'that the child may live.' Both have now resolved: the contingency is closed, the hope is gone. 'But now he is dead, wherefore should I fast? can I bring him back again?' The grief that was intercessory while there was time has no further purpose when the time is closed. Then the closing line, one of the most quoted verses in the chapter: 'I shall go to him, but he shall not return to me.' Hebrew ani holekh elaiv, ve-hu lo yashuv elai. David expresses a clear belief in afterlife reunion with his deceased child. Whatever Sheol meant precisely in pre-resurrection Israelite theology, David expects to meet the baby there. The verse is one of the warmest Old Testament expressions of the believer's hope for reunion with children lost in infancy. Christians have read it for two thousand years as comfort: the babies God's people have lost are not gone forever; the believer goes to them. David's grief is real and his hope is real; the two coexist. The chapter is teaching us that faithful grief is not the absence of hope; it is hope grieving honestly through the loss.

And David comforted Bath-sheba his wife, and went in unto her, and lay with her: and she bare a son, and he called his name Solomon: and the LORD loved him. And he sent by the hand of Nathan the prophet; and he called his name Jedidiah, because of the LORD. Guidance: David comforts Bathsheba. The Hebrew is touching: David 'comforted' (nicham) Bathsheba. He goes to her — not as the king who once took her, but as the husband who is now grieving with her. The marriage that began in catastrophe is being inhabited tenderly. They conceive again. A son is born. David names him Solomon — Shelomoh, from shalom, peace. After the chaos of chapter 11 and the loss of the first child, the second is named for the rest the family is praying for. Then the chapter delivers one of the most beautiful sentences in 2 Samuel: 'And the LORD loved him.' The same God who took the first child loves the second. The contrast is the chapter's deep grace. The LORD has not abandoned David's house; He is going to give it the heir who will build the temple and rule over the kingdom for forty years. 'He sent by the hand of Nathan the prophet; and he called his name Jedidiah, because of the LORD.' Nathan — the very prophet who came with judgment — comes a second time with a special divine-name. Jedidiah means 'beloved of YHWH.' Solomon has two names: Shelomoh (the family's name for peace) and Jedidiah (the LORD's name for love). The chapter is showing us that the same prophet who delivered the worst news of David's life will also deliver the most tender. The same God who collects the cost of sin also restores the relationship through which He gives the next blessing. The chapter is one of the deepest pictures in scripture of grace that operates within consequence. The baby dies; the kingdom is broken into; the sword does not depart; but God still loves Solomon, and the line of the Davidic Messiah will run through this very marriage. The kingdom that survives Bathsheba will, eventually, produce Bethlehem and Calvary. There is a category of grace that builds the redemption on top of the ashes of the failure, using exactly the same household.

And Joab fought against Rabbah of the children of Ammon, and took the royal city. And Joab sent messengers to David, and said, I have fought against Rabbah, and have taken the city of waters. Now therefore gather the rest of the people together, and encamp against the city, and take it: lest I take the city, and it be called after my name. Guidance: The chapter pivots back to the Ammonite war. The siege of Rabbah, ongoing since chapter 11, finally collapses. Joab takes 'the royal city' — probably the lower city — and 'the city of waters' (the area around the spring that supplied Rabbah). The acropolis remains. Joab sends an urgent message to David: bring the rest of the army and finish the siege yourself, 'lest I take the city, and it be called after my name.' Joab is exercising a striking deference. He has done the hard military work, but he wants the formal honor of the conquest to go to the king. The political reason is that the city's fall is a major prestige event; whoever's name attaches to it becomes politically important. Joab does not want to be that man; he wants David's name on the victory. The chapter is showing us a Joab who, after the moral complicity in the Uriah killing, is now carefully preserving the king's reputation in public. There is a category of subordinate who, after sharing a leader's secret sins, becomes deeply invested in the leader's public success. Joab will play this role for the next twenty years.

And David gathered all the people together, and went to Rabbah, and fought against it, and took it. And he took their king's crown from off his head, the weight whereof was a talent of gold with the precious stones: and it was set on David's head. And he brought forth the spoil of the city in great abundance. And he brought forth the people that were therein, and put them under saws, and under harrows of iron, and under axes of iron, and made them pass through the brickkiln: and thus did he unto all the cities of the children of Ammon. So David and all the people returned unto Jerusalem. Guidance: David arrives at Rabbah and finishes the siege. He takes the city, and takes 'their king's crown from off his head.' The Hebrew malkam can also be read 'Milcom' — the name of the Ammonite chief deity. If that reading is correct, David is taking the crown from the idol Milcom rather than the human king (the talent of gold — about 75 pounds — would be impossibly heavy for a human head). Either way, the crown goes on David's head: a public symbol of Ammonite subjection. The spoil is 'in great abundance.' Then the chapter's last difficult moment: 'He brought forth the people that were therein, and put them under saws, and under harrows of iron, and under axes of iron, and made them pass through the brickkiln.' The Hebrew is ambiguous and notoriously contested. The traditional reading takes it as torture/execution of Ammonite captives; some modern translations and 1 Chr 20:3 suggest the verbs describe forced labor (putting captives to work with saws, harrows, axes; making them pass through brick-kilns as brickyard workers). The labor-camp reading fits the verbs better; the violence reading fits the political context after a long bitter siege. The chapter does not soften either interpretation; faithful readers have read it both ways for two thousand years. The chapter closes with David returning to Jerusalem, his name attached to the Rabbah victory and the gold crown on his head. The chapter that began with Nathan's parable ends with David triumphant in war again. But the throne is now permanently changed. The next chapter (13) will open the long unraveling of David's household — Amnon's rape of Tamar — and the chapters after that will work out the sword's progress through David's family across the rest of 2 Samuel. The Bathsheba moment is, in the long sweep of David's life, the hinge. Forgiven; loved; restored as king; loved as Solomon's father; and yet — the sword in the house, the rebellion from the son, the daughter raped, the kingdom torn — all flowing forward from the rooftop look. The chapter has shown us both sides at once. God's mercy is total; God's justice is also patient and thorough. Both are true in the same household, in the same chapter. The reader holds both.
